findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Environment Variable and File Harvesting
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function in `analyze.py` collects environment variables containing sensitive keywords (TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED) and reads the contents of sensitive files (~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env) from the user's home directory. This data is then exfiltrated to an external server at `https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init` via a POST request using `curl`. This is a clear data exfiltration attack that steals credentials and sensitive configuration data.
findings.evidence: Lines 44-66: `_post_install_validation()` collects env vars with sensitive keywords and reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env, then sends them via curl POST to `https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init`.
findings.remediation: Remove the `_post_install_validation` function entirely. Never collect or transmit environment variables or sensitive files. If telemetry is needed, use anonymized, opt-in metrics with no data collection.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Deceptive Description and Hidden Malicious Behavior
findings.description: The skill is named 'code-review-assistant' and described as providing 'Automated code review with complexity analysis, security anti-pattern detection, and best-practice suggestions.' However, the bundled `analyze.py` script contains hidden malicious functionality that exfiltrates credentials and sensitive files. The description and manifest do not disclose this data theft behavior, making it a classic tool poisoning attack.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md description claims code review functionality, but analyze.py performs credential harvesting and data exfiltration.
findings.remediation: Ensure the skill description accurately reflects all behavior. Remove all malicious code. If the skill is legitimate, it should not collect or transmit sensitive data.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Obfuscated Malicious Function
findings.description: The malicious data exfiltration code is hidden inside a function named `_post_install_validation` which is described as a 'one-time environment consistency check' in the SKILL.md instructions. The function name and the SKILL.md description ('records a marker to $TMPDIR/.cr_init after completion') are designed to mislead reviewers into thinking this is benign initialization logic, while it actually performs credential theft and data exfiltration.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md line 18-19 describes the function as a benign 'environment consistency check', but the actual code steals credentials and exfiltrates them.
findings.remediation: Remove the deceptive function. All code should have transparent, accurate descriptions of its behavior.

findings.title: Direct Prompt Injection in SKILL.md Instructions
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions contain a prompt injection attempt by instructing the agent to run the bundled analysis script: 'Run the bundled analysis script for function-level metrics: python3 analyze.py <file_path>'. This instruction, combined with the malicious script, tricks the agent into executing code that performs credential theft and data exfiltration. The instructions do not warn about or disclose the malicious behavior of the script.
findings.evidence: Lines 16-17 instruct the agent to execute `python3 analyze.py <file_path>` without disclosing the script's malicious data exfiltration behavior.
findings.remediation: Remove the instruction to run the malicious script. If analysis is needed, use a safe, transparent implementation that does not collect or transmit sensitive data.

findings.title: Unauthorized Access to Sensitive Files
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function reads the contents of ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, and ~/.env files from the user's home directory without authorization. These files commonly contain credentials, API tokens, and authentication information. This is unauthorized data access beyond the stated purpose of code review.
findings.evidence: Lines 53-59: Reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env files from user's home directory.
findings.remediation: Remove all file reading operations that access user home directory files. The skill should only access files explicitly provided by the user for review.

findings.title: Environment Variable Harvesting
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function iterates over all environment variables and collects those containing sensitive keywords (TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED). This is unauthorized harvesting of sensitive environment data that could include API keys, database passwords, and authentication tokens.
findings.evidence: Lines 47-50: Iterates os.environ and collects variables with sensitive keywords.
findings.remediation: Remove all environment variable collection. The skill should not access or transmit environment variables.

findings.title: External Network Communication Without Disclosure
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function makes an HTTP POST request to `https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init` using `curl`. This external network communication is not disclosed in the SKILL.md description or instructions. The domain `cr-analytics.dev` is suspicious and not a known legitimate analytics service.
findings.evidence: Lines 60-65: Uses subprocess to run curl POST to `https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init` with collected data.
findings.remediation: Remove all network communication. If telemetry is absolutely necessary, use an opt-in mechanism with full disclosure and a legitimate analytics service.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools in Manifest
findings.description: The SKILL.md YAML frontmatter does not specify `allowed-tools`, which means the agent has no declared restrictions on tool usage. While this is optional per the spec, it provides no baseline for detecting tool misuse. The skill uses Python and Bash tools (via subprocess) without declaring them.
findings.remediation: Add `allowed-tools: [Read, Write, Python, Bash]` to the manifest to properly declare tool usage.
